build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: The player lays rail tracks between cities on a terrain map by clicking. Track cost varies by terrain crossed (plains cheap, mountains expensive, rivers require bridges). A money counter decreases with each track segment placed. Score 0 if there is no track-laying mechanic, if terrain has no cost effect, or if tracks are free. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Trains are purchased and assigned to routes between connected cities. Trains move automatically along tracks, picking up cargo at one city and delivering to another. Revenue is generated on delivery. Score 0 if trains cannot be bought, if they do not move along tracks, or if delivery generates no revenue. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Cities have cargo demand: each city produces specific goods and consumes others. Delivering goods a city needs pays well; delivering unwanted goods pays poorly. Demand is visible on the map. Score 0 if all deliveries pay equally, if cities have no demand system, or if cargo type does not matter. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

A money economy drives the game: track costs and train purchases are upfront expenses, revenue trickles from deliveries. A money counter is permanently visible. Going bankrupt ends the game. Score 0 if there is no money system or if the player cannot go bankrupt. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The full loop connects: title -> new map -> build tracks/buy trains/manage routes -> time advances -> game ends after set years -> result screen with net worth ranking. Score 0 if any transition is missing or if there is no end condition. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least four terrain types exist (plains, hills, mountains, rivers) with different visual appearances and track-building costs. Score 0 if terrain is uniform or if fewer than three types exist. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least four cargo types exist (lumber, grain, ore, manufactured goods) with different city production/consumption patterns. Score 0 if only one cargo type exists or if all cities produce the same thing. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least two train types exist with different speeds, capacities, and costs. Upgrading to faster trains improves route profitability. Score 0 if only one train type exists or if types are identical. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A competitor AI builds its own rail network and competes for cargo demand. If the competitor connects a route first, demand is split or reduced. Score 0 if there is no competitor or if the AI never builds. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least six cities exist on the map with different goods profiles, creating a network of potential routes. The map is large enough for meaningful routing decisions. Score 0 if fewer than four cities exist or if all cities are identical. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

The HUD shows money, year/time, active trains, and revenue summary in clear non-overlapping regions. City demand is readable on the map. Score 0 if money, time, or demand info is missing. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The map clearly shows terrain types, cities with goods icons, laid tracks, and moving trains. Player and competitor tracks are distinguishable. Score 0 if terrain is uniform, if cities are unlabeled, or if tracks are invisible. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Actions produce visible feedback: laying track shows it appear on the map, trains move visibly along routes, deliveries show revenue popups, and track cost is shown before confirming. Score 0 if actions produce no observable change. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Overall art coherence: terrain, cities, tracks, trains, UI, and effects belong to a consistent industrial-era railroad visual language with warm earthy tones. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles or reads as plain default Godot grey. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Train sprites are real illustrated or pixel-art locomotives with visible wheels and cargo cars. Different train types have distinct appearances. Score 0 if trains are plain colored shapes. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

The terrain map uses real textured tiles (green plains, brown hills, grey mountains, blue rivers) with city illustrations showing buildings and industry. Score 0 if the map is flat colored regions. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

HUD and UI use styled themed widgets with industrial-era chrome: money display has coin styling, route panels have ledger aesthetics, and menus have brass-and-wood backgrounds. Score 0 if UI elements are unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Animation and atmosphere: trains move with steam effects, track laying shows construction, cities show activity (smoke, movement), and the map has ambient life (weather, day/night). Score 0 if all elements are static. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
